name: Jardine
short_description: High-elevation BLM rockhounding site in the Paradise Valley area near Yellowstone with quartz and agate specimens. Personal use collecting allowed on public land with no permit required.
description:
Jardine sits at 8574 feet elevation in the northern Paradise Valley region of Montana, positioned on BLM public land that offers classic high-country rockhounding. The site occupies gentle slopes characteristic of the Basin and Range transition zone where Tertiary-age volcanic rocks and hydrothermal alteration have produced quartz-rich deposits, agates, and jasper specimens scattered across exposed outcrops and hillsides.

The area benefits from relatively accessible terrain compared to other high-elevation Montana sites, with parking near the community of Jardine and short hikes to productive collecting zones. Specimens found here range from small thumb-sized agates to larger quartz clusters weathering out of volcanic parent material. The setting provides excellent views toward the Absaroka Range and opportunities to observe the geological processes that created these mineral deposits.

As BLM land managed for multiple uses, Jardine welcomes recreational rockhounders under the standard 25-pound-per-day personal use allowance. The high elevation and seasonal accessibility make this a destination for serious collectors during the brief summer and early fall window when mountain roads are clear and weather permits extended time at elevation.

Visitors should expect to walk gentle but rocky slopes with exposed volcanic material underfoot. The community of Jardine provides a historic reference point; the site itself is remote enough to feel like genuine backcountry collecting, yet accessible enough for determined day trips from Gardiner or Livingston.
history: This area sits within the northern Absaroka volcanic field where Eocene to Miocene rhyolitic volcanism and associated hydrothermal fluids created silica-rich mineral deposits that crystallized as quartz, agate, and jasper. The gentle slopes and exposed outcrops represent weathered volcanic deposits that have been worked informally by local rockhounders for decades as part of the historic gemstone collecting tradition across the Paradise Valley region.
tips: Search along hillside exposures and wash banks where weathering has freed agate and quartz specimens from their volcanic host rock; look for translucent white, gray, and banded specimens ranging from 1-3 inches across. Bring a 3-5 pound crack hammer and cold chisel to work specimens out of softer altered zones around quartz veins; avoid heavy pounding on the hardest rhyolite as it wastes energy. The high elevation means afternoon thunderstorms develop quickly, so plan collecting sessions for early morning and mid-day, then retreat by mid-afternoon.
terrain_notes: Elevation 8574 feet with gentle slopes and exposed volcanic bedrock. Terrain is rocky with volcanic rubble and scattered talus; footing is stable but requires careful stepping. Short walks from parking area to collecting zones, typically under 1 mile of hiking. Vegetation sparse above treeline with some low brush and scattered conifers. High-elevation exposure means rapid weather changes and potential afternoon storms.
best_season: June - September. High elevation means heavy snow closes access from October through May. Summer and early fall offer the only reliable window for safe road access and weather-stable collecting. July and August provide longest daylight and most stable conditions, though afternoon thunderstorms are common.
written_directions: From Gardiner, Montana, take US 89 south approximately 8 miles toward Mammoth. Turn west onto a BLM access road near the Paradise Valley area (consult current BLM maps for exact turnoff as road access may vary seasonally). Continue on rough but passable dirt road for approximately 4-6 miles climbing toward Jardine. Passenger vehicles can typically reach lower collecting zones, though high-clearance or 4x4 vehicles are recommended for upper access points. Cell service is spotty; download offline maps before departing Gardiner.
road_conditions: Rough dirt/gravel BLM road with washboard surface and occasional high-clearance sections. Seasonal mud and snow present October-May. Summer conditions passable with awd vehicle.
parking_notes: Informal pullout areas along the BLM access road. Limited formal parking; expect to find dispersed parking along road shoulders. No developed lot facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. BLM land collecting permits not required for recreational use. Respect other uses and leave site in good condition.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Recreational collecting permitted without permit
Remove trash and respect the site
nearest_services: Gardiner, Montana approximately 8-10 miles south; gas, food, water, and lodging available. Livingston, Montana approximately 50 miles north for additional services.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with rocky slopes and volcanic rubble. Walking required on uneven ground with scattered loose rock underfoot. Sturdy hiking boots essential. High elevation (8574 ft) may cause exertion; acclimatize before vigorous activity. No wheelchair access. Short walks from parking minimize overall difficulty for fit adults and older children.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Jardine, Montana?
faq.answer: Jardine is known for quartz specimens, agates with gray and white banding, jasper, and feldspar. Most specimens range from thumb-sized agates to palm-sized quartz clusters weathering out of the volcanic host rock. The high-elevation volcanic setting produces excellent-quality agate with translucent and banded characteristics.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Jardine on BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at Jardine. Montana BLM public land allows personal use collecting under FLPMA regulations. You are limited to 25 pounds per day, and commercial collection is prohibited. Simply follow the access road to the site and begin collecting.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to visit Jardine for rockhounding?
faq.answer: June through September is the only reliable collecting window. High elevation (8574 feet) means heavy snow blocks access from October through May. July and August offer the most stable weather and longest daylight hours, though afternoon thunderstorms are common and you should plan to leave by mid-afternoon.

faq.question: How do I reach Jardine from Gardiner, and what vehicle do I need?
faq.answer: From Gardiner, take US 89 south approximately 8 miles, then turn west on a BLM access road toward the Paradise Valley area. The rough dirt road climbs 4-6 miles to Jardine. An awd vehicle is recommended for safety and reliability; high-clearance is essential. Passenger cars may reach lower collecting zones but risk damage on washboard sections.

faq.question: What tools and techniques work best for collecting agates and quartz at Jardine?
faq.answer: Bring a 3-5 pound crack hammer and cold chisel to work specimens out of softer altered zones around quartz veins. Search along exposed hillside outcrops and wash banks where weathering has freed specimens. Avoid heavy pounding on the hardest rhyolite as it wastes effort. Look for translucent white and gray material and banded agate patterns.

faq.question: Is Jardine beginner-friendly for family rockhounding trips?
faq.answer: Yes, Jardine is suitable for beginners and families with children age 8 and older. The gentle slopes and short walks from parking make it accessible to fit families. No special skills or expensive equipment are required. The high elevation and afternoon thunderstorms mean careful planning and early starts are essential for safety.

meta_title: Jardine Rockhounding - Montana Agate and Quartz
meta_description: Find agate, quartz, and jasper at Jardine, Montana on BLM public land. No permit required, 25 lb/day limit. High-elevation collecting June-September.
